464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member Onboarding**: no documented first-touch-to-first-class journey; inconsistent goal-setting, fitness assessment, payment setup across staff
- **Member Checkins & Retention**: no cadence for proactive outreach to at-risk members; no win-back protocol for paused/lapsed
- **Cancellation & Churn**: no documented exit interview process; no last-chance intervention SOP before member leaves
- **Class Quality Audit Checklist**: no standardised way to measure coaching consistency, safety, energy, client experience week-to-week
- **Weekly Key Coach Tasks**: no clarity on what coaches must do beyond teaching (spotting, form corrections, member check-ins, feedback logs)
- **New Quarter Program Launch**: no SOP for rolling out seasonal themes, class structures, or transformation tracks
- **Member Outcomes & Milestone Tracking**: no process to capture, celebrate, or report progress (strength gains, transformations, retention metrics)
- **Referral Programme**: no documented mechanics (incentive structure, tracking, fulfillment, comms)
- **Troubleshooting Guide**: no decision tree for common issues (booking errors, billing disputes, complaint escalation, coach conflicts)
- **Order Supplies & Sundries**: no restocking trigger, vendor approval, budget controls, receiving checklist
- **Member Data Privacy & GDPR**: no data handling, consent, retention, or breach response SOP (legal & compliance risk)

**Needs updating**

- **Coaching Development Roadmap**: likely exists but is static; needs quarterly review cycle, progression milestones, and feedback loop from class audits back into coach development
- **Transformation Pillars**: unclear if tied to actual member outcomes tracking or just marketing; needs integration with onboarding goal-setting and checkin SOP
- **Facilities (5

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE — INCREASE

- **AR/M (Average Revenue per Member) is collapsing.** June £120.48 vs May £165.56 (−27%). With 122→147 members (+25 members), you're not pricing upsells or retention correctly. Each new member is adding less value. Action: audit what the 25 new members signed up for (intro rates?), and test a £3–5 price rise on renewals + mandatory add-ons (PT intro session, class bundles).
- **Churn spiked in April (8.33%) and May (5.74%) then recovered.** June 0% churn is excellent but fragile. With studio doubled in size and member base up 37% since May, you're not yet anchoring them. Action: implement a 30-day onboarding sequence (personal goal-setting, class buddy assignment, 1:1 trainer check-in at day 15) to lock retention above 2%.
- **December revenue spike (£21.1k, 49.54% profit) proves your model scales hard.** May–June dropped to £20.2k→£17.7k (−12% M-o-M). New studio should pull higher baseline. Action: run a "new studio opening" promotion in July–August (free week trial for corporate/group bookings) to fill off-peak morning/midweek classes and drive recurring revenue.

## EXPENSES — REDUCE

- **Non-people costs in April–May were grossly inflated by move; June still £9,390 vs pre-move baseline of £4.3–4.9k.** That's £4.5–5k of drift. Action: itemise June non-people spend immediately—utilities, insurance, cleaning, supplies. New studio is ~2× size; some increase is real, but isolate one-off vs recurring overspend. Negotiate utilities/landlord and consolidate supplies vendors (target: £6.2k/month by August).
- **People costs rising steadily: £3,116 (Feb)→£3,686 (June), +18% in 4 months.** Active clients only +42 (+42 members, +3.4 per month). Payroll-to-revenue ratio deteriorated (Feb 19.7%→June 20.8%). Action: measure staff hours per member served. If you've not added trainers/class capacity, hours are inefficient. If you have, enforce a "1 trainer per 35 active members" ratio and freeze hiring until AR/M recovers to £

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: Her 17 Build: Lower, 9 Build: Upper, 6 Elevate and 3 Strongevity sessions need homes. JoJo already leads Build: Upper (15 sessions) — add Grace's 9 Upper classes to her; split Build: Lower between JoJo and Eilis; assign Elevate and Strongevity to Eilis to broaden her class range and free up her PT slots.

• **Rebalance JoJo's PT load**: She's carrying 44 PT sessions and 20 scans (64 billable hours in practice) plus heavy class load. Move 10–15 of her PT clients to Eilis (who has only 11 PT currently) to reduce burnout risk and develop Eilis as a PT coach.

• **Use Eilis's PT capacity**: She's under-loaded on 1:1s. Bring her PT volume up to 20–25 sessions/month while keeping her off Tuesdays, which will ease JoJo's workload and give Eilis income-generating work.

• **Delegate all Body Composition scans to admin or reception**: These are not coaching and are inflating the team's "delivery" hours. Scans should never block a coach's calendar — free up 40 scan-hours per month across the team for actual coaching.

• **Plug the Circuits and Strongevity gap**: These niche classes sit with only JoJo (6 and 3 sessions). Once Eilis takes Strongevity from Grace, teach her Circuits as well to avoid single-person dependency and give JoJo breathing room.

• **Target: Grace to zero by month-end**: Confirm her 52 June sessions are reassigned by end of July. This should lower team total delivery

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio gaps:**

1. **Open-gym blocks on Thursday evenings and Saturday mornings** — these are your quietest days with likely dead time between PT slots. Charge a small drop-in fee (£5-8) to let members use the studio unsupervised; captures revenue from people who want flexibility without committing to a full class.

2. **Small-group PT packages marketed to Barbelle, Strongevity, and Games Night regulars** — these three classes have collapsed (12-14 attendees in 30 days). Use the quiet Monday/Thursday/Saturday gaps to offer 3-person semi-private sessions at £25-35pp, positioning it as "exclusive coaching for niche training styles" rather than group classes.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move Engine to Wednesday evening and rebrand it as "cardio for strength athletes"** — Engine is severely underattended (15 in 30 days), but Wednesday is your busiest day. Position it as active recovery or conditioning *for* your Build class regulars, and invite Build attendees directly. If Wednesday doesn't fit the timetable, trial a Thursday lunchtime slot to capture different demand patterns.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

• **Audit before you build** — Have your top 3 coaches list every service they currently sell/mention (formally or casually). You'll find inconsistencies and discover upsells you're already doing but not systematizing. This becomes your actual menu, not theoretical offerings.

• **Price anchor to your best performer** — Identify which service/package generates the highest margin or repeat bookings. Build your menu structure around that (e.g., if small group PT converts best, make it prominent and price other services relative to it). Don't price in a vacuum.

• **Create TWO menus with different language** — Coach version lists margin, upsell sequences, and objection handlers ("if member says 'too expensive,' pivot to 6-week block discount"). Member version strips this; it's benefit-focused and includes social proof (e.g., "most popular with members training for competitions").

• **Embed it into your booking system TODAY** — Don't create a PDF and file it. Integrate into your Mindbody/booking app or create a simple WhatsApp-friendly price sheet coaches can screenshot and send. Friction kills adoption; make it their reflex.

• **Set a 2-week coach feedback loop** — Push it live, observe what questions come up, what they avoid selling, what members ask about. You'll have real data to refine by month-end, not guesswork.

What's your current booking system?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
# The Forge — July Performance Analysis & Recommendations

## Insights

**Your lead cost has exploded 4.5x from April to July (£6 → £27) while volume has collapsed 94% (69 → 4 leads).** This isn't a minor efficiency dip; you're getting catastrophically fewer leads at exponentially higher cost, despite July spend being lowest. The close rate held strong (75% in July vs 29% in April), so creative/offer resonance isn't the problem—reach and relevance are broken.

**Spending less money but getting almost no leads suggests your ad account or targeting has degraded severely.** Either Meta's algorithm has throttled you (account quality, creative fatigue, audience saturation, or policy issues), or you've accidentally narrowed targeting so tight you're reaching almost no one. The profitable profit margin in July (£458 on £109 spend) masks a dying campaign.

**The real warning: April's 69 leads on £435 (£6/lead, 29% close = 20 sales) was your benchmark. You're now at 1/17th of April's volume.** If July spend had matched April's £435 budget with current CPL, you'd spend £4,680 to get 16 leads and ~4 sales—underwater fast.

---

## Actions

1. **Audit account health immediately.** Check Meta Ads Manager for: policy violations or account restrictions (even soft ones), ad relevance diagnostics (Quality Ranking, Engagement Rate Ranking), pixel tracking (is conversion data flowing?), and audience overlap/saturation (are you bidding against yourself?). If you see "Limited Reach" or low Quality Rank, you have a structural problem, not a budget problem.

2. **Resurrect April's winning setup—creatives, targeting, placement.** Pull the exact ad creative, copy, CTA, and audience from April; test it against July's. If April version works again, you've proven it's *current* creative/targeting fatigue. Rebuild with fresh creatives (new footage, testimonial-driven, offer-heavy), test lookalike audiences off your 20 April converters, and exclude anyone who's seen ads >3 times in

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Actions (June 2026 snapshot)

## REVENUE — IMMEDIATE PRIORITY

• **Rev/member has collapsed to £107 (June) from £138 (April).** This 22% drop is your biggest red flag. You've added 25 members (122→147) but revenue per head fell. Root cause: mix shift to cheaper packages or intro pricing not converting. Audit your pricing tiers immediately — if you're running heavy discounts to fill the new studio post-relocation, tighten or sunset them now. Target: £130+ per member within 60 days.

• **Churn spiked to 11.9% (May) after the move, then settled to 3.2% (June).** Good recovery. But the May spike cost ~13 members and £1,500+ revenue. Post-relocation comms and a "welcome back" retention campaign should have been simultaneous. For next growth phase, pre-announce venue moves 6 weeks ahead to existing members and offer a 1-month loyalty discount to lock them in during transition.

• **Peak classes are likely running full; quiet slots are dead revenue.** With 147 members and (estimate) 4–5 peak hours/day, your 06:00 and 16:00 slots probably hit 15–20 people while 10:00–14:00 sits at 2–4. Launch a "lunch-break power hour" promo (£8 drop-in or new "off-peak" monthly tier at £35/month) and target local office workers. Projected uplift: £400–600/month.

## EXPENSES — SECONDARY PRIORITY  

• **Cash out in June (£20.6k) is 31% above revenue (£15.8k).** Excluding April's relocation, your last 3 months (May–June) averaged 18% cash burn month-on-month. Major leaks: likely rent (new studio space is bigger, assume 20–30% uplift), utilities, and payroll. Request an itemised P&L from your bookkeeper, split by: rent, staff (hours/rates), equipment/maintenance, marketing, and utilities. Identify the top 2 cost lines and flag them here.

• **If rent jumped on relocation, negotiate a 3-month free/reduced rent period as a tenant improvement credit** — standard in commercial lettings. Save £1.5–3k/month. If already locked in, sublet a corner desk (£150–300/month) or partner with a local PT to use the space Sundays (rent-share).

• **
